content:
{
  "title": "Fact-Chain Prompting: Inductive Chaining for Factual Consistency in LLMs",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) often generate hallucinated facts or unverifiable statements, reducing their trustworthiness in knowledge-critical applications. Existing prompting methods struggle to ensure strict adherence to factual information.",
  "motivation": "Improving factuality in LLM outputs is crucial for applications in science, healthcare, and education, where misinformation can have real consequences. With the increasing adoption of LLMs, robust methods to reduce hallucinations are urgently needed.",
  "method": "We propose Fact-Chain Prompting, which decomposes user queries into explicit chains of sub-facts via structured prompts. The LLM is first prompted to enumerate the minimal set of verifiable sub-facts necessary to answer the query, and then sequentially prompted to verify each sub-fact against an external knowledge base (via API calls or retrieval-augmented prompts). The final answer is synthesized only if all sub-facts are validated, and the answer prompt is conditioned on the validated fact chain. This explicit chaining and verification mechanism encourages the LLM to ground its generations in checkable facts, reducing hallucination.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate Fact-Chain Prompting on the TruthfulQA, FEVER, and HotpotQA datasets. Baselines will include standard zero-shot, chain-of-thought, and retrieval-augmented prompting. Metrics will cover factual accuracy, hallucination rate, and answer completeness, benchmarked using human and automated evaluators."
}
